metadata.title: Monte Carlo Portfolio Simulation — 10,000 GBM Paths with Claude
metadata.note: CLI video spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 slate · B09 outro
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

You put a hundred thousand dollars into a diversified portfolio and leave it for thirty years. What do you get? You do not know. The best answer finance has is a probability distribution over outcomes — and the tool that generates it is Monte Carlo simulation. Ten thousand random walks through time, each one a plausible path for the portfolio under geometric Brownian motion. Today we build it with Claude, vectorized with NumPy, and read the fan of possible futures.
beats.visual_intent: title card: fan of GBM paths diverging over time, median vs P5/P95

beats.narration_text: Type the prompt. We ask Claude to simulate ten thousand GBM paths for thirty years of monthly steps, mu 7 percent, sigma 15 percent, initial value 100k. Print the percentile table — P5, P25, median, P75, P95 — year by year. And report the runtime.

props.command:
claude "Write monte_carlo_portfolio.py: simulate 10,000 GBM paths
for mu=0.07, sigma=0.15, V0=100000, T=30 years, monthly steps
using vectorized numpy. Print table: Year | P5 | P25 | Median
| P75 | P95. Report runtime."

beats.narration_text: Read it. The core is one vectorized line — the log-normal return per step. Mu minus half sigma squared times dt is the drift; sigma root dt times a standard normal is the shock. Cumsum compounds the returns; exp converts log-returns to levels. Ten thousand paths, three hundred sixty steps — no loop.

props.code:
# monte_carlo_portfolio.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np, time
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
n, T, steps = 10_000, 30, 360
dt = T / steps
mu, sigma, V0 = 0.07, 0.15, 100_000

t0 = time.perf_counter()
returns = (mu - 0.5*sigma**2)*dt + sigma*np.sqrt(dt)*rng.normal(0,1,(n, steps))
paths = V0 * np.exp(np.cumsum(returns, axis=1))  # shape (10000, 360)
print(f'Runtime: {time.perf_counter()-t0:.2f}s')

beats.narration_text: Run it. The grey lines are 20 representative paths — some paths triple in value, some stall. The crimson line is the median path — it ends near eight hundred thousand after thirty years. The dashed green line is the P95 path; the dashed red is P5. Five percent of the time you end below two hundred thousand. The spread is what risk looks like.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: 20 GBM paths as thin grey lines, median in crimson, P5 dashed red, P95 dashed green, over 30 years

beats.narration_text: The model assumes constant volatility. That is wrong — volatility clusters. Ask Claude to add a regime switch: during recession years, sigma doubles for 12 months. How much does the P5 outcome change?

props.command:
claude "update monte_carlo_portfolio.py:
  -> add recession regime: 20% probability per year
  -> during recession, sigma doubles for 12 months
  -> compare P5 outcome: base vs regime-switching"

beats.narration_text: Regime switching drops the P5 outcome by roughly 40 percent. The same median, but the tail is much fatter. That is the simulation answer: the distribution, not the point estimate, is the product. And the shape of the tail is where risk management actually lives.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: P5 comparison table base vs regime-switching; tail risk illustration

beats.narration_text: Your move: add withdrawals. Simulate a 4 percent annual withdrawal from the portfolio each year and find the probability of ruin — running out before thirty years. That is the retirement planning question that Monte Carlo was built to answer.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: probability of ruin vs withdrawal rate; 4% rule illustration

beats.narration_text: Then add multiple assets. A two-asset portfolio with correlation rho — simulate the correlated Brownian motions using Cholesky decomposition and watch how correlation changes the spread of outcomes.
beats.visual_intent: code snippet: Cholesky decomp for correlated GBM paths

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
